Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. Being 27 and having been through some life experiences, I knew I needed discipline. I'd block out about five hours a day across the week, mostly in the mornings when my mind was clearest. I'd read through materials, take notes by hand, and then type them up later. It wasn't perfect, but I needed that consistency to manage my anxiety about falling behind.
Q2: I aimed for regularity, but life doesn't always cooperate. Early in the semester I had set times, but as deadlines piled up, everything shifted. Some weeks I'd be studying late into the night before a submission, other weeks I'd be more relaxed. My anxiety would spike when the pattern broke, so I tried to get back on track quickly. The deadlines definitely dictated more than I wanted them to.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or seeing if grades had been posted. That anticipation of feedback, even when I dreaded it, pulled me in. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't missed a deadline because that fear was always there. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped too—seeing progress bars and achievements gave me a small push to check in more often.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in became almost automatic, like checking email. The gamified elements helped with that—I'd want to see if I'd earned any new badges or completed a streak. It wasn't deeply engaging, but it became habitual. I'd open the LMS with my morning coffee, skim through what was new, and plan my day around it. That routine kept me from spiraling when my anxiety got bad.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were my lifeline. I learn best by reading and writing things out, so PDFs and text-based resources clicked with me. I'd download them, annotate them, and create my own summaries. The gamified task system was surprisingly helpful too—it broke things into smaller chunks that felt manageable. When you're anxious, small wins matter a lot.
Q6: I'll be honest, I barely touched the discussion forums. With only four posts the whole course, that tells you something. I found them overwhelming—too many voices, too much pressure to say something smart. The videos I'd watch once, maybe at 1.5x speed, but I never rewatched them. I preferred having text I could control, highlight, and revisit at my own pace without the time pressure of video.
Q7: If I could see a clear connection between the activity and my grade or my understanding, I was in. The gamified tasks worked because they showed me exactly what completing them would achieve. I needed that clarity. Vague activities that felt like busywork? Those got pushed aside. At 27, I don't have patience for things that feel like they're wasting my time—I need purpose.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps for my anxiety, honestly. If I did them, I'd worry I was spending time away from required work. If I skipped them, I'd worry I was missing something important. Required activities had clear stakes, which paradoxically made them easier to approach. I knew exactly what would happen if I didn't do them. Optional stuff left too much uncertainty.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could, print some materials, and work from my desk with handwritten notes. I have good resources at home, so I'd use my own textbooks and external articles to supplement. The LMS was more of a content delivery system for me—the actual learning happened with pen and paper, away from the screen.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be deep in study, working through concepts, making flashcards, rewriting notes—and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd downloaded the week's materials on Monday, and then my LMS activity would go silent while I was actually doing the hard cognitive work. My exam preparation especially looked invisible because it was all offline review.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a PDF or reading once, download it immediately, and never open it in the LMS again. My task completion rate was decent, but the way I completed tasks was mostly offline. I'd read the downloaded materials multiple times, mark them up, but the system only saw that single initial access. It's just how I work best—I need materials I can control.
Q12: Around midterms, my anxiety peaked badly. I was juggling 19 online courses worth of content, my attendance had slipped, and I felt like I was drowning. There were days I'd stare at the LMS and feel paralyzed. The gamified elements actually helped pull me back—small achievable tasks when everything else felt impossible. But those rough patches definitely showed in my engagement dips.
Q13: Fatigue was huge. Studying 34 hours a week while managing high anxiety is exhausting. Some weeks I'd log in, see the mountain of work, and just close the tab. Competing responsibilities—life stuff, you know—would pull me away. When I was overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS and focus my remaining energy on assignments that directly affected my grade.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other deadlines or just zoning out from exhaustion. I'd scroll through readings without absorbing anything. The LMS would log my time, but I wasn't really there mentally. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to check a box.
Q15: The discussion posts, definitely. I knew participation mattered, but the anxiety of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge was paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, then later became never. Even some readings I'd postpone because they felt dense and I didn't have the mental energy. Avoidance became a coping mechanism, even though I knew it made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary motivator and my primary stressor. I'd structure everything around them—what was due soonest got attention first. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before submissions. It wasn't ideal, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety paralysis. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift indefinitely.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback boosted my mood significantly—that post-intervention high feeling. But waiting for feedback was torture, and negative feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral. I'd sometimes delay checking grades because I was scared of what I'd see. When feedback was constructive and specific, though, it genuinely shaped how I approached the next assignment.
Q18: The structure helped more than I expected. Having clear modules, defined deadlines, and the gamified task system gave me scaffolding to work with. Without structure, my anxiety would have me spinning in circles. The course gave me enough framework to function, even if my engagement level stayed relatively low. I needed those external boundaries because my internal ones were shaky.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—breaking things into smaller pieces with visible progress helped my anxious brain cope. But the discussion requirements made it harder. Forcing interaction when you're anxious about judgment is rough. The video-heavy content was also challenging for my read-write learning style. I wished there were more text-based alternatives for the visual content.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly, clicking through things, trying to engage but not being able to focus enough to actually produce work. High anxiety can make you circle around tasks without completing them. You're present but paralyzed. The activity looks like engagement, but it's really just anxious checking and rechecking without progress.
Q21: That resonates with me completely. My LMS engagement was low, but I passed and my assignment scores were decent. The real work happened offline, in my notes, in my head during walks, in conversations with myself working through concepts. Low login frequency doesn't mean low effort—it might mean someone found an efficient system that doesn't require constant platform presence.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. That's useful for identifying students who've disappeared entirely. But logs miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental effort. They can't see me wrestling with a concept at my desk for hours. They measure presence, not learning. There's a huge gap between activity and understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means the material has my genuine attention and I'm actively trying to understand and retain it. It's not just showing up—it's caring about what I'm learning and putting mental effort into connecting ideas. For me, engagement often looks quiet and internal. It's the difference between reading words and actually thinking about what they mean and how they fit together.
Q24: My anxiety management strategies, for one. The mental effort it takes me to even open the LMS some days. The hours of offline study that actually drove my learning. The way the gamified tasks gave me small wins that kept me going when I wanted to quit. The emotional labor of being a 27-year-old trying to climb toward something better despite internal obstacles. None of that shows in click data.
